step2_rag_chunks: If you have tried to quit before, that experience is actually useful information â€” not a failure. What worked even a little? What made it harder? Most people who successfully quit have tried several times before it sticks. Each attempt teaches you something about your own pattern.
Relapse can happen during the quitting process. Instead of feeling discouraged, treat it as a learning experience and continue trying to quit.
Withdrawal symptoms usually peak within the first few days after quitting and gradually decrease over time.

step4_session_phase.phase_instruction: You are in the MOTIVATION phase. You understand their triggers. Now explore their motivation: why do they want to quit — is it health, family, cost, doctor advice? Have they tried before and what happened? Be empathetic. Do NOT push strategies yet.

turns.user_message: It was the constant meetings and tight deadlines. I felt like I was going to lose my job over it. And my manager was breathing down my neck. That anxiety just took over, I guess.
step2_rag_chunks: There are two kinds of triggers â€” situational and emotional. Situational triggers are external: after meals, with tea, during a work break. Emotional triggers are internal: stress, boredom, loneliness, anxiety. Most people have both. Knowing which type is stronger for you helps us choose the right approach.

It is completely normal to want to quit and also feel unsure about it at the same time. Most people feel this way â€” one part of them wants to stop, another part is not ready. That conflict is not weakness, it is just how change works. It helps to talk about both sides openly rather than pushing the doubts aside.
Relapse can happen during the quitting process. Instead of feeling discouraged, treat it as a learning experience and continue trying to quit.

Patient: I guess I could take a few deep breaths before the meeting, but what if it's a super important one and I have to go right in? And what if I have to make a presentation? My anxiety is going to be through the roof. This is all just a lot to think about.

Therapist: It sounds like you're feeling overwhelmed thinking about all the different situations where you might need to manage your anxiety and cravings. Can you imagine practicing those deep breaths in a low-stakes moment, like when you're waiting for a meeting to start, so you feel more confident and prepared for when you actually need them?

Patient: Yeah, I could try taking those deep breaths when I'm waiting in the conference room before a meeting. It's not as high-pressure as being in the meeting itself. Maybe if I practice it enough, it'll start to feel more natural and I'll be able to do it even when things get tough.
Current patient message:

Yeah, I could try taking those deep breaths when I'm waiting in the conference room before a meeting. It's not as high-pressure as being in the meeting itself. Maybe if I practice it enough, it'll start to feel more natural and I'll be able to do it even when things get tough.
[RESPONSE]
Therapist:
step7_response: That's a good start, and it sounds like you're feeling more hopeful about being able to manage your anxiety and cravings. Can you think of a physical activity that you've enjoyed in the past, like drawing or using a stress ball, to keep your hands busy during meetings when you might normally reach for a cigarette?

turns.user_message: I used to doodle a lot in meetings, before everyone started having meetings with their laptops open. I liked drawing the weird architectural designs on the conference room walls. Maybe I could pick up drawing again, or even bring a stress ball to the meeting. It's not the same as quitting, but it's something, right?
step2_rag_chunks: Making a daily plan for managing cravings can make the quitting process easier.

If you have tried to quit before, that experience is actually useful information â€” not a failure. What worked even a little? What made it harder? Most people who successfully quit have tried several times before it sticks. Each attempt teaches you something about your own pattern.
